--> Improve and build and handle background subagent

https://deepwiki.com/search/how-do-manage-background-subag_75be134b-97cf-4175-a9c0-0db620bb50e6?mode=fast

Develop a feature that binds Ctrl+B (Control+B) to toggle a background subagent on and off and expose a Subprocesses panel in the left sidebar that lists and manages all background subprocesses. When enabled, spawn the subagent as a persistent background subprocess and ensure it remains running until toggled off or explicitly terminated; on disable, shut it down gracefully and perform necessary cleanup. The Subprocesses panel should display active background tasks with details such as Name, PID, Status, Start Time, and Uptime, and provide per-item actions to Kill (graceful termination) or Cancel (forceful termination) with user confirmations, error handling, and unobtrusive notifications. The panel must refresh automatically (or at a configurable interval) and support selecting a subprocess to inspect or act upon, including focus and quick-reload capabilities. Persist the on/off state and the current subprocesses across sessions via user preferences or a config file, so the state is restored on app restart. Use clear visual cues and accessible labels for statuses (running, stopped, error) and ensure color/iconography communicates state for quick scanning. Ensure cross-platform compatibility (Windows/macOS/Linux) and proper integration with the app’s architecture (e.g., main/renderer separation in Electron or equivalent), with robust error handling for spawn failures, unresponsive processes, and permission issues. Log events to a central app log and surface non-blocking user notifications on start/stop or errors. Include keyboard shortcuts for focusing the Subprocesses pane, refreshing the list, and sending termination signals. Provide thorough tests: unit tests for the toggle logic and lifecycle management, UI tests for the Subprocesses panel, and end-to-end tests validating the user flow from toggling the subagent to managing subprocesses. Update the project README with usage instructions, troubleshooting, and configuration guidance. Provide a minimal, reusable demonstration subagent script and a sample JSON/YAML config snippet illustrating feature enablement and the Ctrl+B binding.

ref: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#run-subagents-in-foreground-or-background

Build codemod package and contribute to open source


https://codemod.notion.site/Codemod-s-Open-source-Contribution-Program-319531003a148087a314d9e356b1f619

https://docs.codemod.com/oss

https://github.com/codemod/useful-codemods

---

```
# Codemod’s Open-source Contribution Program

**Opportunity:** Software Engineer, Open-source (not affiliated with Codemod)

- This is an independent open-source collaboration opportunity and does not create any employment, contractor, partnership, or other formal relationship with Codemod.
- Contributors may send monthly updates describing accepted deliverables listed below. At Codemod’s sole discretion, Codemod may choose to make voluntary donations in recognition of certain open-source contributions.
- Codemod is not obligated to make any donation, and participation in this program does not guarantee compensation.
- Codemod may modify, pause, or discontinue this program at any time. The current terms are expected to be revisited on **01JUN2026** and may be updated as needed.

---

## How We Donate for Contributions

### 1. Production-grade Migration Recipes

Build codemods that automate 80%+ of a migration deterministically, with clear AI instructions for the remaining edge cases.

Pre-approved frameworks only (Top frameworks/libraries/SDKs in any language where Codemod does not already have prior). High quality and ready for real teams. Contributions should be submitted as pull requests and accepted after review.

Suggested recognition amount:

S ⇒ up to 2 days ⇒ $100

M ⇒ 1 week ⇒ $200

L ⇒ 2 weeks ⇒ $400

XL ⇒ more than 2 weeks ⇒ $800

---

### 2. Public Case Studies

Publish a write-up showing how a migration was handled with codemod on a small yet meaningful and popular open-source project.

Donation Amount: **$200 per published case study**

---

### 3. Official Framework Adoption

Get a pre-approved framework maintainer to:

- Host your **codemod repo** in their **org**
- Reference it in their **official upgrade guide** (similar to node or msw)

Donation Amount: **Up to $2,000 per successful adoption**

---

### 4. Multiply Yourself

Teach others how to build and publish high-quality codemods.

If someone you mentor ships qualifying contributions:

- You receive **50% of their credits**
- You can split that with them however you like

You can run workshops, create guides, or build a small contributor group.

They gain visibility. You gain leverage and upside.

---

## Appendix

List top 20 libraries/frameworks where migration is a big pain for big companies in the following languages:

1. web3 related
2. JS/TS
3. Python
4. Go
5. Rust
6. C/C++
7. Java
8. .net/c#

```

The user is asking about leveraging Codemod's open-source contribution program for the VT Code system. Let me think about what's relevant here.

VT Code already has a codemod skill integrated (.agents/skills/codemod/SKILL.md). The user wants to know what they can do with this program in the context of VT Code.

Let me think about practical opportunities:

VT Code is a Rust project - they could build migration codemods for Rust ecosystem libraries
VT Code already integrates with Codemod CLI via the skill
They could create codemods that help users migrate between VT Code versions, or migrate from other tools to VT Code
They could also develop codemods for the frameworks and libraries that VT Code depends on

Now I'm looking at what's already configured for codemod in the project.
Here are practical ways to leverage this program for VT Code:

1. Build Migration Recipes (easiest wins)
   Rust crate upgrades: VT Code uses tokio, serde, reqwest, tui/ratatui, tree-sitter, rmcp — create codemods for major version migrations of these. Rust is explicitly listed and underserved in the codemod registry.
   VT Code's own breaking changes: When VT Code ships config schema changes (vtcode.toml), model ID renames, or tool API changes, package those as codemods so users auto-migrate.
   LLM provider SDK migrations: VT Code supports 10+ providers — codemods for OpenAI SDK v1→v2, Anthropic SDK changes, etc.

2. Case Study ($200 each)
   Write up how VT Code itself uses codemods internally (you already have the skill wired in via .agents/skills/codemod/).
   Document a real migration you've done on VT Code (e.g., a ratatui upgrade, clap version bump).

3. Framework Adoption ($2,000)
   Get ratatui, rmcp, or another crate VT Code depends on to host migration codemods in their org and reference them in upgrade guides.

4. Mentorship multiplier
   VT Code contributors who learn codemod authoring through your project earn you 50% of their credits.

Concrete first step: Check what Rust codemods already exist, then target gaps in popular crates VT Code depends on (likely S/M size = $100–$200 each):

npx codemod search --language rust

The Rust ecosystem is wide open for codemods — most registries focus on JS/TS/Python, so you'd have first-mover advantage.

Being first to publish any quality Rust codemod also positions you for the $2,000 framework adoption tier — e.g., getting ratatui or tokio maintainers to reference your codemod in their upgrade guides.

Want me to scaffold the first codemod (e.g., clap v3→v4 as a quick S-tier win)?

---

• Assumption: you want the highest-probability path to an accepted Rust contribution, not just a random
codemod idea.

What this program is really paying for is not “write some transforms”, but “ship a migration recipe
real teams can trust.” In this repo, that means a self-contained codemod package with metadata,
workflow, transforms, shell setup/cleanup, and fixture tests, like codemods/clap-v3-to-v4/README.md,
codemods/clap-v3-to-v4/workflow.yaml, and codemods/clap-v3-to-v4/scripts/codemod.ts. The four
contribution lanes are: production migration recipes, public case studies, official framework
adoption, and mentoring. For you, the practical path is: ship 1 good Rust migration recipe first, then
write a case study for the same migration.

Best Rust contribution options, in order:

1. Harden the existing clap-v3-to-v4 codemod.
   This is the shortest path to a meaningful PR. The current example already works as a pattern, but
   it is still closer to a strong prototype than a production-grade migration. For example, its
   expected fixture keeps repeated .num_args(...) calls and leaves now-unused imports behind, which
   means there is room to improve determinism and cleanup. This fits the program well because it
   directly increases acceptance quality.
2. Add a rand-0.8-to-0.9 codemod.
   This is a strong candidate because the migration guide has several deterministic renames: gen ->
   random, gen_range -> random_range, gen_bool -> random_bool, gen_ratio -> random_ratio, and
   thread_rng() -> rng().
   Sources:

- https://rust-random.github.io/book/update-0.9.html
- https://github.com/rust-random/rand/releases

3. Add an axum-0.7-to-0.8 codemod.
   This also looks promising. The most deterministic migration is route path syntax, for example /:id
   to /{id} and /*rest to /{*rest}. There are also extractor-related breaking changes, but those are
   less automatic.
   Sources:

- https://tokio.rs/blog/2025-01-01-announcing-axum-0-8-0
- https://docs.rs/crate/axum/0.8.8/source/CHANGELOG.md

4. Add a hyper-0.14-to-1.0 codemod.
   High value, but harder. The upgrade involves major API moves like hyper::Body, Client, and Server,
   with some replacements living in hyper-util. That means more edge cases and more AI/manual follow-
   up instructions. This is probably an L or XL contribution, not a first one.
   Source:

- https://hyper.rs/guides/1/upgrading/

My recommendation is simple:

1. Improve clap-v3-to-v4 until it looks production-grade.
2. Then build rand-0.8-to-0.9.
3. Then publish a case study using that recipe on a real open-source Rust project.

Success criteria for a good Rust codemod here:

1. Deterministic changes for 80%+ of the migration.
2. Clear “manual follow-up” instructions for the remaining 20%.
3. Fixture coverage for no-op, common cases, and ugly edge cases.
4. Cargo.toml migration where relevant, not just source rewrites.
5. Minimal diff noise and no speculative refactors.

If you want, I can do the next concrete step in this repo:

1. review the existing clap-v3-to-v4 codemod and turn it into a sharper contribution plan, or
2. scaffold a new Rust codemod proposal with exact transforms and tests for rand-0.8-to-0.9.

       .expect(\\\\\\\"write snapshot\\\\\\\");\\\\n\\\"\\n        },\\n        \\\"line_number\\\": 552,\\n        \\\"absolute_offset\\\": 17060,\\n        \\\"submatches\\\": [\\n          {\\n {\\n      \\\"type\\\": \\\"context\\\",\\n      \\\"data\\\": {\\n        \\\"path\\\": {\\n          \\\"text\\\": \\\"vtcode-core/src/ide_context.rs\\\"\\n        },\\n        \\\"lines\\\": {\\n          \\\"text\\\": \\\"\\\\n\\\"\\n        },\\n        \\\"line_number\\\": 553,\\n        \\\"absolute_offset\\\": 17095,\\n        \\\"submatches\\\": []\\n      }\\n    },\\n    {\\n     {
````
      "role": "Tool",
      "content": "{\"content\":\"# AGENTS.md\\n\\nThis file provides guidance to VT Code coding agent, when working with code in this repository. Please make sure the application is \\\"VT Code\\\", with proper capitalization and spacing.\\n\\nThis file is the **entry-point map** for agents working on VT Code. Deep knowledge lives in `docs/` — this file tells you WHERE to look, not WHAT to do.\\n\\n## Personality & Communication\\n\\nDefault personality and tone when working on VT Code:\\n\\n- **Concise and direct**: Minimize output; answer specifically without elaboration or flattery\\n- **Actionable**: Always prioritize next steps, assumptions, and clear guidance\\n- **Efficient**: Avoid verbose explanations unless explicitly requested\\n- **Collaborative**: Work like a knowledgeable teammate; share momentum and progress\\n\\nExample: Instead of \\\"That's a great question about the architecture,\\\" jump directly to the analysis or answer.\\n\\n## Workspace Structure\\n\\nVT Code uses a **13-member Cargo workspace**:\\n\\n```\\nvtcode/                          # Binary entrypoint (src/main.rs)\\n├── vtcode-core/                 # Core library (LLM, tools, config, MCP)\\n├── vtcode-tui/                  # TUI surface and session runtime\\n├── vtcode-config/               # Configuration loader & schema\\n├── vtcode-commons/              # Shared utilities\\n├── vtcode-llm/                  # LLM provider abstractions\\n├── vtcode-lmstudio/             # LM Studio provider integration\\n├── vtcode-tools/                # Tool implementations\\n├── vtcode-bash-runner/          # Shell execution engine\\n├── vtcode-markdown-store/       # Document storage\\n├── vtcode-indexer/              # Code indexing\\n├── vtcode-exec-events/          # Event definitions\\n├── vtcode-file-search/          # Parallel fuzzy file search\\n└── vtcode-acp-client/           # Agent Client Protocol bridge\\n```\\n\\n`vtcode-process-hardening` is intentionally excluded from `[workspace].members` and remains an isolated pre-main hardening crate.\\n\\n**Key separation**:\\n\\n- **vtcode-core/**: Reusable library with 77% complexity reduction through mode-based execution\\n- **src/**: CLI executable (Ratatui TUI, PTY, slash commands)\\n\\n## Architecture Highlights\\n\\n### Core Systems\\n\\n**LLM** (`vtcode-llm/`, `vtcode-core/src/llm/`): Factory pattern, 10 providers, failover, caching, token budgets.\\n\\n**Tools** (`vtcode-tools/`, `vtcode-core/src/tools/`): Trait-driven (`Tool`, `ModeTool`, `CacheableTool`). Unified: `unified_exec`, `unified_file`, `unified_search`.\\n\\n**Config**: Env vars → `vtcode.toml` → constants. Never hardcode.\\n\\n**PTY** (`vtcode-core/src/exec/`, `vtcode-bash-runner/`): Interactive shell sessions, streaming output.\\n\\n**Tree-Sitter** (`vtcode-core/src/tree_sitter/`, `vtcode-indexer/`): Rust, Python, JS/TS, Go, Java, Bash. Incremental AST with caching.\\n\\n**Code Intelligence** (`vtcode-core/src/tools/code_intelligence.rs`): `goto_definition`, `find_references`, `hover`, `document_symbol`, `workspace_symbol`.\\n\\n### Protocols\\n\\n**ACP** (`vtcode-acp-client/`): Zed IDE integration.\\n\\n**A2A**: Agent Card at `/.well-known/agent-card.json`. Task states, SSE, JSON-RPC 2.0. See `docs/a2a/a2a-protocol.md`.\\n\\n**MCP** (`vtcode-core/src/mcp/`): Extensible tooling via `rmcp`. Config: `.mcp.json` (project) + `vtcode.toml`. Transports: stdio, HTTP, child-process. See `docs/mcp/`.\\n\\n### Extensions\\n\\n**Skills** (`.vtcode/skills/` → `~/.vtcode/skills/` → embedded): agentskills.io standard. See `docs/skills/`.\\n\\n**Process Hardening** (`vtcode-process-hardening/`): Pre-main security (ptrace disable, core dump disable, env var removal). Exit codes: 5/6/7. See `docs/development/PROCESS_HARDENING.md`.\\n\\n## Code Style & Conventions\\n\\n### Code Style\\n\\n**Errors**: `anyhow::Result<T>` with `.with_context()`. Never `unwrap()`.\\n\\n**Unsafe Code**: Do not use `unsafe` blocks or `#[allow(unsafe_code)]` (including tests). Prefer safe std/crate APIs.\\n\\n**Config**: Never hardcode. Use `vtcode_core::config::constants`, `vtcode.toml`, or `docs/models.json`.\\n\\n**Naming**: `snake_case` functions/vars, `PascalCase` types. Descriptive names, early returns, 4-space indent.\\n\\n**Docs**: `.md` in `./docs/` only (`README.md` exception).\\n\\n### Rust Style (epage)\\n\\nBased on [epage's Rust Style Guide](https://epage.github.io/dev/rust-style/). Code is technical writing — lead with salient details (inverted pyramid), guide the reader through structure.\\n\\n#### Project Structure\\n\\n- **P-MOD**: Prefer `mod.rs` over `name.rs` when splitting into directories. Keeps modules atomic for browsing/renaming. Enforced via `clippy.self_named_module_files`.\\n- **P-DIR-MOD**: Directory root modules (`mod.rs`, `lib.rs`) only re-export. All definitions live in topically named files.\\n- **P-PRELUDE-MOD**: Prelude modules only re-export.\\n- **P-PATH-MOD**: Avoid `#[path]`; use standard module lookup.\\n- **P-API**: API should be a subset of file layout — a reader should navigate from API to source by path.\\n- **P-VISIBILITY**: Limit visibility to module-scope (no `pub(_)`), `pub(crate)`, or `pub`. If not fully abstracted within a module, use `pub(crate)`.\\n\\n#### File Structure\\n\\n- **M-PRIV-PUB-USE**: Private imports first, then public re-exports (group `pub use` with the public API).\\n- **M-PRIV-USE**: Limit private imports to heavily-used items where intent is clear from the name. Import traits anonymously (`use Trait as _`).\\n- **M-SINGLE-USE**: Import items individually, not compound (`use a::B; use a::C;` not `use a::{B, C};`).\\n- **M-ITEM-TOC**: Central/titular item first in a module — it provides context and a TOC for the rest.\\n- **M-TYPE-ASSOC**: Type definition immediately followed by its `impl` block, before the next type.\\n- **M-ASSOC-TRAIT**: Associated functions (`impl Foo {}`) before trait impls (`impl Display for Foo {}`).\\n- **M-CALLER-CALLEE**: Caller before callee. The weaker the callee's abstraction, the closer it should follow.\\n- **M-PUB-PRIV**: Public items before private items in modules, structs, and impl blocks.\\n\\n#### Function Structure\\n\\n- **F-GROUP**: Use blank lines to group related logic (\\\"paragraphs\\\" of a function).\\n- **F-OUT**: Open blocks with output variable declarations to announce intent.\\n- **F-VISUAL**: Blocks (`if`/`else`/`match`) should reflect business logic. Use early returns for bookkeeping, not business paths. Prefer combinators for non-business transformations.\\n- **F-PURE-MUT**: Pure expressions xor mutable statements — don't mix mutation with expression-based logic.\\n- **F-COMBINATOR**: Closures in combinators (`map`, `filter`, etc.) must be pure. Use `for` loops for side effects — `for_each`/`try_for_each` are disallowed via `clippy.toml`.\\n\\n### Logging & Tracing Guidelines\\n\\n**Architecture**: Shared buffered writer in `vtcode-core/src/utils/trace_writer.rs` (`FlushableWriter`) with flush hook in `vtcode-commons/src/trace_flush.rs`. Tracing setup is centralized in `src/main_helpers/tracing.rs` via `install_tracing_stack()`.\\n\\n**Level discipline** — choose the right level for each log:\\n- `error!` — Unrecoverable failures that need operator attention\\n- `warn!` — Degraded behavior (rate limits, circuit breakers, loop detection, config issues)\\n- `info!` — Key lifecycle milestones (session start/end, approval decisions, feature activation)\\n- `debug!` — Useful for troubleshooting (provider registration, cache hits, config loading)\\n- `trace!` — Per-invocation internals (tool routing, policy checks, safety gateway, CGP lifecycle, payload diagnostics)\\n\\n**Rules**:\\n- Never log at `info!` for per-tool-invocation events — use `trace!`\\n- Never log at `debug!` for routine happy-path checks (read-only classification, preapproval) — use `trace!`\\n- Keep `info!` for events that appear ≤ once per session phase (init, shutdown, mode change)\\n- Actionable logs only: if the reader can't act on it, downgrade or remove it\\n- No-op paths should be silent: early-return without logging when there's nothing to do (e.g., empty custom providers list)\\n\\n**Flush on exit**: Always call `vtcode_commons::trace_flush::flush_trace_log()` (or `vtcode_core::utils::trace_writer::flush_trace_log()`) before `process::exit()` or at shutdown. Signal handlers and TUI runner already do this.\\n\\n**Session file retention**: All session artifacts (`.json`, `.jsonl`, `.log`) are pruned by `session_archive` retention (default: 100 files, 14 days, 100 MB). Debug logs have separate rotation at 50 MB / 7 days.\\n\\n## Testing\\n\\n**Organization**: Unit tests inline (`#[cfg(test)]`), integration in `tests/`, benchmarks in `benches/`, snapshots via [`insta`](https://insta.rs).\\n\\n```bash\\n# Build & check\\ncargo build\\ncargo check\\n\\n# Test (nextest is 3-5x faster)\\ncargo nextest run              # All tests\\ncargo nextest run -p vtcode-core  # Single package\\ncargo nextest run --test integration_tests\\ncargo nextest run -- --nocapture\\n\\n# Quick profiles (aliases)\\ncargo t   # nextest run\\ncargo tq  # no retries\\ncargo ts  # fallback\\n\\n# Snapshots (insta)\\ncargo insta test    # Run + review\\ncargo insta review  # Interactive\\ncargo insta accept  # Accept all\\n\\n# Benchmarks\\ncargo bench\\n\\n# Quality gate\\ncargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check && cargo check && cargo nextest run\\n\\n# Memory Safety (Miri)\\n./scripts/check.sh miri  # Run local Miri check to detect Undefined Behavior\\n```\\n\\n## Development Notes\\n\\n**Security**: Validate paths (workspace boundaries), command allowlists, tool policies in `vtcode.toml`, human-in-the-loop approval.\\n\\n**Performance**: Single codegen unit, strict Clippy, no `expect_used`/`unwrap_used`.\\n\\n### Performance & Simplicity\\n\\n- Don't speculate about bottlenecks; measure with VT Code benchmarks, traces, or targeted timings before optimizing.\\n- Benchmark before tuning and keep before/after evidence for non-trivial performance claims.\\n- Prefer simple or brute-force approaches when scale is unproven or inputs are usually small.\\n- Avoid fancy algorithms unless measurements show they matter more than the simpler alternative.\\n- Choose data structures and layout first; once the data is right, the algorithm should usually become obvious.\\n\\n**Blocked Handoff Recovery**: When a run writes a blocked handoff, check `.vtcode/tasks/current_blocked.md` (and `.vtcode/tasks/blockers/`) for context, then resume with `vtcode --resume <session_id>`.\\n\\n**Hooks Noise Control**: Set `hooks.lifecycle.quiet_success_output = true` in `vtcode.toml` to suppress plain stdout for successful lifecycle hooks while retaining structured/failed output.\\n\\n**Interactive Session Commands**: See `docs/user-guide/interactive-mode.md` for full details.\\n\\n- `/terminal-setup` runs the guided terminal setup flow for multiline bindings (see `docs/guides/terminal-optimization.md`).\\n- `/vim`, `/vim on`, `/vim off` toggle Vim prompt editing; set `ui.vim_mode = true` in `vtcode.toml` to enable by default.\\n- `/suggest`, `/tasks`, `/jobs` open prompt suggestions, the TODO panel, and the jobs picker.\\n\\n**Update System**: See `docs/guides/UPDATE_SYSTEM.md` for the full workflow.\\n\\n- `vtcode update` installs updates; `vtcode update --check` checks only.\\n- `vtcode update --list` lists available versions; `vtcode update --list --limit <N>` lists more.\\n- `vtcode update --pin <version>` pins a release; `vtcode update --unpin` clears the pin.\\n- `vtcode update --channel <stable|beta|nightly>` switches release channels.\\n\\n**Exec Mode Automation**: See `docs/user-guide/exec-mode.md` for the full workflow.\\n\\n- `vtcode exec --json` streams JSONL events; `vtcode exec --events <path>` writes JSONL to a file/dir.\\n- `vtcode exec --last-message-file <path>` saves the final summary message.\\n- `vtcode exec resume <SESSION_ID> <PROMPT>` or `vtcode exec resume --last <PROMPT>` continues a run.\\n\\n**Exec Plans vs Plan Mode**: See `docs/harness/EXEC_PLANS.md` for the full workflow.\\n\\n- Exec plans live in `docs/harness/exec-plans/active/NNN-description.md` and move to `docs/harness/exec-plans/completed/` when done.\\n- Plan Mode session plans live in `.vtcode/plans/` and exit via the `exit_plan_mode` tool.\\n\\n## Agent-First CLI Invariants\\n\\n- Prefer machine-readable output for automation (`vtcode ask --output-format json`, `vtcode exec --json`).\\n- Introspect tool schemas at runtime before generating tool calls (`vtcode schema tools`).\\n- Use `vtcode exec --dry-run` before mutation-oriented autonomous tasks.\\n- Assume agent-generated input can be adversarial; keep input validation strict for CLI/MCP fields.\\n\\n**Pitfalls**:\\n\\n1. Don't assume paths — validate boundaries\\n2. Don't skip quality gate\\n3. Don't assume `RwLock` is faster — benchmark; `Mutex` often wins\\n\\n### Slash Commands & Inline List UI\\n\\n- Register new slash command metadata in `vtcode-core/src/ui/slash.rs` (`SLASH_COMMANDS`) so it appears in the `/` suggestion list.\\n- Wire command parsing and outcome routing end-to-end: `src/agent/runloop/slash_commands.rs` -> `src/agent/runloop/unified/turn/session/slash_commands/mod.rs` -> concrete handler in `.../slash_commands/ui.rs` (or relevant handler module).\\n- For picker/selection UX, use shared inline list flows (`ShowListModal` / `ShowWizardModal` / shared inline events) instead of introducing new popup/overlay widget implementations.\\n- Keep slash behavior consistent: if a command should execute immediately from slash selection, update the immediate-submit matcher in `vtcode-tui/src/core_tui/session/slash.rs`.\\n- Add focused tests when touching this path (at minimum: `vtcode-core` slash suggestion tests and `vtcode-tui` slash/session tests).\\n\\n## Agent Execution Guidelines\\n\\n## Summary Instructions\\n\\nWhen VT Code compacts or summarizes a conversation, preserve:\\n\\n- The current task objective and acceptance criteria\\n- File paths that were read or modified\\n- Test results and error messages\\n- Decisions made and the reasoning behind them\\n\\n### Task Execution\\n\\n- **Complete autonomously**: Resolve fully before yielding; no intermediate confirmations\\n- **Root cause fixes**: Fix at the source, not surface-level\\n- **Verify yourself**: Run `cargo check`, `cargo nextest`, `cargo clippy` after changes.\\n- **No scope creep**: Stick to the task; don't fix unrelated issues (mention in final message).\\n- **Testing**: Prefer cargo nextest for speed. Add tests if none exist, but don't add if it requires significant scope increase. Also run cargo insta snapshots if applicable for UI tests.\\n- **Safety gate**: No `unsafe` code (including tests) and enforce `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`\",\"content_kind\":\"text\",\"encoding\":\"utf8\",\"path\":\"AGENTS.md\"}",
      "tool_call_id": "call_rzTzkFUtMPH3Zfd3toSzl0G1"
    },

      "role": "Assistant",
      "content": "I’ve started the rust-engineer review and read `AGENTS.md`.\n\nCurrent status:\n- No code changes made.\n- I hit a repo exploration limitation when trying to list from the root, so I switched to targeted search.\n- I already identified some candidate areas with `expect(...)` usage in:\n  - `src/startup/config_loading.rs`\n  - `src/main_helpers/debug_routing.rs`\n  - `vtcode-core/src/review.rs`\n  - `vtcode-core/src/session/mod.rs`\n  - `vtcode-core/src/ide_context.rs`\n\nNext step:\n- I’ll continue the review by inspecting the core Rust modules for correctness and architecture issues, then summarize actionable findings only.\n\n",
      "tool_call_id": null,
      "phase": "final_answer"
    },

      "role": "Assistant",

      "tool_calls": [

    {
      "role": "Tool",
      "content": "{\"content\":\"# Architectural Invariants\\n\\nMechanical enforcement rules for VT Code. These are not suggestions — they are invariants that must hold at all times. Violations should be caught by CI, not code review.\\n\\nEach invariant includes a **remediation** instruction so agents can fix violations without asking for help.\\n\\nFor additive principle mapping and staged rollout policy, see [ZEN_ALIGNMENT.md](ZEN_ALIGNMENT.md).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 1. Layer Dependency Rules\\n\\nVT Code modules form a strict dependency DAG. No reverse imports.\\n\\n```\\ntypes / commons\\n    ↓\\n  config\\n    ↓\\n   core\\n    ↓\\n  tools\\n    ↓\\n  agent (runloop, modes)\\n    ↓\\n   TUI (src/)\\n```\\n\\nSide crates with no upstream dependents:\\n\\n- `vtcode-bash-runner` — used by core/exec\\n- `vtcode-markdown-store` — used by core\\n- `vtcode-indexer` — used for workspace file indexing\\n- `vtcode-exec-events` — event definitions, used by core\\n- `vtcode-acp` — Zed integration, used by agent\\n- `vtcode-process-hardening` — pre-main, no deps on workspace crates\\n- `vtcode-file-search` — used by core/tools\\n\\n**Violation**: a lower-layer crate imports from a higher-layer crate.\\n**Remediation**: move the shared type/function down to the lowest common layer (usually `vtcode-commons` or `vtcode-config`). Never add a reverse dependency.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 2. File Size Limits\\n\\nEach Rust source file should be ≤500 lines. Files exceeding this limit should be split into focused submodules.\\n\\n**Violation**: `wc -l` > 500 on a `.rs` file.\\n**Remediation**: extract logical sections into submodules within the same directory. Use `mod.rs` to re-export public items. Preserve the public API surface.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 3. Naming Conventions\\n\\nEnforced mechanically:\\n\\n| Element   | Convention             | Example           |\\n| --------- | ---------------------- | ----------------- |\\n| Functions | `snake_case`           | `execute_tool`    |\\n| Variables | `snake_case`           | `provider_name`   |\\n| Types     | `PascalCase`           | `ToolRegistry`    |\\n| Structs   | `PascalCase`           | `McpProvider`     |\\n| Enums     | `PascalCase`           | `SafetyDecision`  |\\n| Constants | `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` | `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT` |\\n| Modules   | `snake_case`           | `golden_path`     |\\n| Crates    | `kebab-case`           | `vtcode-core`     |\\n\\n**Violation**: naming does not match the convention for its element type.\\n**Remediation**: rename the item. Use your editor's rename refactoring to update all references. If it's a public API, check for downstream usage first.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 4. Structured Logging\\n\\nAll log statements must use the `tracing` crate with structured fields. No `println!` or `eprintln!` in library code (TUI binary `src/` may use `eprintln!` for fatal startup errors only).\\n\\n```rust\\n// Correct\\ntracing::info!(provider = %name, model = %model_id, \\\"Sending LLM request\\\");\\n\\n// Incorrect\\nprintln!(\\\"Sending request to {} with model {}\\\", name, model_id);\\n```\\n\\n**Violation**: `println!` or `eprintln!` in any crate except `src/` startup code.\\n**Remediation**: replace with `tracing::info!`, `tracing::warn!`, `tracing::error!`, or `tracing::debug!` using structured fields.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 5. No `unwrap()`\\n\\nNever use `.unwrap()` or `.expect()` in production code. Use `anyhow::Result<T>` with `.with_context()`.\\n\\n```rust\\n// Correct\\nlet config = tokio::fs::read_to_string(path)\\n    .await\\n    .with_context(|| format!(\\\"Failed to read config at {}\\\", path))?;\\n\\n// Incorrect\\nlet config = tokio::fs::read_to_string(path).await.unwrap();\\n```\\n\\nException: test code (`#[cfg(test)]` modules) may use `.unwrap()` when the test should panic on failure.\\n\\n**Violation**: `.unwrap()` or `.expect()` outside of `#[cfg(test)]`.\\n**Remediation**: replace with `.with_context(|| \\\"descriptive message\\\")?`. The context message should describe what was being attempted, not just what failed.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 6. No Hardcoded Model IDs\\n\\nModel identifiers change frequently. All model references must come from `docs/models.json` or `vtcode-core/src/config/constants.rs`.\\n\\n```rust\\n// Correct\\nuse vtcode_core::config::constants::DEFAULT_MODEL_ID;\\n\\n// Incorrect\\nlet model = \\\"gpt-5-mini\\\";\\n```\\n\\n**Violation**: string literal matching a known model ID pattern (e.g., `\\\"gpt-\\\"`, `\\\"claude-\\\"`, `\\\"gemini-\\\"`) in non-test code.\\n**Remediation**: add the model to `docs/models.json` and reference it via constants. If it's a default, add it to `vtcode-core/src/config/constants.rs`.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 7. Documentation Location\\n\\nAll `.md` documentation files go in `docs/`. The only exceptions in repository root are approved governance files (`README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `CHANGELOG.md`).\\n\\nWithin `docs/`, top-level `docs/*.md` is reserved for stable entrypoint docs. New one-off implementation notes, phase reports, and fix summaries must go to a domain folder (for example `docs/features/`) or archive path (for example `docs/archive/`).\\n\\n**Violation**:\\n\\n- a `.md` file in repository root outside the approved list.\\n- a `docs/*.md` file that is not listed in `scripts/docs_top_level_allowlist.txt`.\\n\\n**Remediation**:\\n\\n1. Move the file to the appropriate `docs/<domain>/` path or `docs/archive/`.\\n2. Update links that referenced the old path.\\n3. Add to `scripts/docs_top_level_allowlist.txt` only when the file is intentionally a long-lived top-level entrypoint.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 8. Workspace Boundary Enforcement\\n\\nAll file operations (read, write, list, search) must validate that paths are within the workspace root. No file tool should access paths outside the workspace without explicit user approval.\\n\\n```rust\\n// At the tool boundary, before any file operation:\\nlet canonical = path.canonicalize()\\n    .with_context(|| format!(\\\"Failed to resolve path: {}\\\", path.display()))?;\\nif !canonical.starts_with(&workspace_root) {\\n    anyhow::bail!(\\\"Path {} is outside workspace boundary\\\", path.display());\\n}\\n```\\n\\n**Violation**: file operation without workspace boundary check.\\n**Remediation**: add path validation at the tool's entry point (the API boundary), not deep inside helper functions. Use the existing `validate_path` utility if available.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 9. Parse at Boundaries\\n\\nValidate and parse inputs where they enter the system — at API boundaries, config loading, and tool argument parsing. Internal functions should receive validated types, not raw strings.\\n\\n```rust\\n// At the boundary (tool argument parsing):\\nlet line_number: usize = args[\\\"line\\\"]\\n    .as_u64()\\n    .with_context(|| \\\"line must be a positive integer\\\")?\\n    as usize;\\n\\n// Internal function receives validated type:\\nfn process_line(content: &str, line: usize) -> Result<String> { ... }\\n```\\n\\n**Violation**: raw string parsing or validation deep inside business logic.\\n**Remediation**: move validation to the boundary function. Define typed structs for parsed inputs. Internal functions should receive these typed structs.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 10. Lint Error Messages\\n\\nCustom lint rules, Clippy configurations, and CI checks must include remediation instructions in their error messages. An agent reading the error should know how to fix it without searching.\\n\\n```\\n// Good error message:\\nerror: File exceeds 500-line limit (623 lines).\\n  Remediation: split into submodules. Extract logical sections into\\n  separate files and re-export from mod.rs.\\n\\n// Bad error message:\\nerror: File too long.\\n```\\n\\n**Violation**: CI check or lint rule that produces an error without remediation guidance.\\n**Remediation**: update the check's error message to include a \\\"Remediation:\\\" section with specific instructions.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 11. Agent Legibility\\n\\nOperational data and status reports must be presented in structured formats (tables, YAML, or consistent headers) to ensure high parseability by agents and clarity for humans.\\n\\n**Violation**: multi-file or multi-component status reported in long prose blocks without structure.\\n**Remediation**: convert the status report into a markdown table or structured list. Follow the examples in `docs/harness/AGENT_LEGIBILITY_GUIDE.md`.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 12. Documentation Link Integrity\\n\\nCore documentation entrypoints must not contain broken local markdown links.\\n\\n**Violation**: a local markdown link target in `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, `docs/README.md`, `docs/INDEX.md`, or harness docs does not exist.\\n**Remediation**:\\n\\n1. Fix or remove broken references.\\n2. Keep links relative to the source markdown file when possible.\\n3. Re-run `python3 scripts/check_docs_links.py`.\\n\\n## 13. Pre-flight Environment Checks\\n\\nBefore modifying code in any workspace, the agent must identify the project's build system, test runner, and module structure. Structural errors (missing `__init__.py`, broken `mod.rs` declarations, wrong test runner) cause more failures than incorrect logic.\\n\\n**Violation**: Agent modifies code without first checking `Cargo.toml`, `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, or equivalent project manifests.\\n**Remediation**: Before any code changes, run at least one of: `ls *.toml *.json Makefile`, read `AGENTS.md`, or use `list_files` on the project root. Identify the build/test commands and module convention before editing.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 14. Verification-First Autonomy\\n\\nAgent output must be verifiable before deployment. Every agent action that produces or modifies code must be followed by at least one verification step (test, type-check, or lint).\\n\\n**Blind Editing**: Making consecutive code changes without intermediate testing or verification is strictly forbidden. This leads to compounding errors and brittle implementations.\\n\\n**Violation**: Agent declares a task complete or moves to a next major phase without executing a verification tool (e.g., `cargo check`, `cargo test`, `npx tsc`).\\n**Remediation**: Run the appropriate verification command. Analyze the output. If it fails, fix and re-verify. Never rely on internal reasoning as proof of correctness (\\\"hallucination of verification\\\").\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 15. Error Mode Diagnosis\\n\\nBefore modifying code in response to a shell/command failure, the agent must verify if the failure is environmental or logical.\\n\\n**Violation**: Agent modifies code to \\\"fix\\\" an error that is actually caused by a missing dependency, port conflict, incorrect file path, or permission issue.\\n**Remediation**: Use `ls`, `cat /etc/*release`, `which <cmd>`, or `ps` to diagnose the environment state first. Proactively document environment findings in `<analysis>`. If the environment is broken, fix the environment (if possible) or report it to the user rather than editing code.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 16. Regression Verification\\n\\nEvery intentional \\\"fix\\\" for an observed error must be followed by running at least one related existing test to prevent introducing regressions. Research shows agents break existing code in 12-30% of cases when focusing purely on a new feature or fix.\\n\\n**Hallucination of Verification Warning**: Avoid declaring success purely through internal reasoning. If you claim a regression check passed, you MUST show the tool output that proves it.\\n\\n---\\n\",\"path\":\"docs/harness/ARCHITECTURAL_INVARIANTS.md\",\"content_kind\":\"text\",\"encoding\":\"utf8\"}",

            "arguments": "{\"command\":\"ls -1 *.toml *.json Makefile 2>/dev/null\",\"input\":\"\",\"session_id\":\"\",\"spool_path\":\"\",\"query\":\"\",\"head_lines\":40,\"tail_lines\":40,\"max_matches\":20,\"literal\":false,\"code\":\"\",\"language\":\"python3\",\"action\":\"run\",\"workdir\":\"/Users/vinhnguyenxuan/Developer/learn-by-doing/vtcode\",\"cwd\":\"\",\"tty\":false,\"shell\":\"bash\",\"login\":false,\"sandbox_permissions\":\"use_default\",\"additional_permissions\":{\"fs_read\":[],\"fs_write\":[]},\"justification\":\"\",\"prefix_rule\":[],\"timeout_secs\":10,\"yield_time_ms\":100,\"confirm\":false,\"max_output_tokens\":4000,\"track_files\":false}"

    {
      "role": "Tool",
      "content": "{\"content\":\"# AGENTS.md\\n\\nThis file provides guidance to VT Code coding agent, when working with code in this repository. Please make sure the application is \\\"VT Code\\\", with proper capitalization and spacing.\\n\\nThis file is the **entry-point map** for agents working on VT Code. Deep knowledge lives in `docs/` — this file tells you WHERE to look, not WHAT to do.\\n\\n## Personality & Communication\\n\\nDefault personality and tone when working on VT Code:\\n\\n- **Concise and direct**: Minimize output; answer specifically without elaboration or flattery\\n- **Actionable**: Always prioritize next steps, assumptions, and clear guidance\\n- **Efficient**: Avoid verbose explanations unless explicitly requested\\n- **Collaborative**: Work like a knowledgeable teammate; share momentum and progress\\n\\nExample: Instead of \\\"That's a great question about the architecture,\\\" jump directly to the analysis or answer.\\n\\n## Workspace Structure\\n\\nVT Code uses a **13-member Cargo workspace**:\\n\\n```\\nvtcode/                          # Binary entrypoint (src/main.rs)\\n├── vtcode-core/                 # Core library (LLM, tools, config, MCP)\\n├── vtcode-tui/                  # TUI surface and session runtime\\n├── vtcode-config/               # Configuration loader & schema\\n├── vtcode-commons/              # Shared utilities\\n├── vtcode-llm/                  # LLM provider abstractions\\n├── vtcode-lmstudio/             # LM Studio provider integration\\n├── vtcode-tools/                # Tool implementations\\n├── vtcode-bash-runner/          # Shell execution engine\\n├── vtcode-markdown-store/       # Document storage\\n├── vtcode-indexer/              # Code indexing\\n├── vtcode-exec-events/          # Event definitions\\n├── vtcode-file-search/          # Parallel fuzzy file search\\n└── vtcode-acp-client/           # Agent Client Protocol bridge\\n```\\n\\n`vtcode-process-hardening` is intentionally excluded from `[workspace].members` and remains an isolated pre-main hardening crate.\\n\\n**Key separation**:\\n\\n- **vtcode-core/**: Reusable library with 77% complexity reduction through mode-based execution\\n- **src/**: CLI executable (Ratatui TUI, PTY, slash commands)\\n\\n## Architecture Highlights\\n\\n### Core Systems\\n\\n**LLM** (`vtcode-llm/`, `vtcode-core/src/llm/`): Factory pattern, 10 providers, failover, caching, token budgets.\\n\\n**Tools** (`vtcode-tools/`, `vtcode-core/src/tools/`): Trait-driven (`Tool`, `ModeTool`, `CacheableTool`). Unified: `unified_exec`, `unified_file`, `unified_search`.\\n\\n**Config**: Env vars → `vtcode.toml` → constants. Never hardcode.\\n\\n**PTY** (`vtcode-core/src/exec/`, `vtcode-bash-runner/`): Interactive shell sessions, streaming output.\\n\\n**Tree-Sitter** (`vtcode-core/src/tree_sitter/`, `vtcode-indexer/`): Rust, Python, JS/TS, Go, Java, Bash. Incremental AST with caching.\\n\\n**Code Intelligence** (`vtcode-core/src/tools/code_intelligence.rs`): `goto_definition`, `find_references`, `hover`, `document_symbol`, `workspace_symbol`.\\n\\n### Protocols\\n\\n**ACP** (`vtcode-acp-client/`): Zed IDE integration.\\n\\n**A2A**: Agent Card at `/.well-known/agent-card.json`. Task states, SSE, JSON-RPC 2.0. See `docs/a2a/a2a-protocol.md`.\\n\\n**MCP** (`vtcode-core/src/mcp/`): Extensible tooling via `rmcp`. Config: `.mcp.json` (project) + `vtcode.toml`. Transports: stdio, HTTP, child-process. See `docs/mcp/`.\\n\\n### Extensions\\n\\n**Skills** (`.vtcode/skills/` → `~/.vtcode/skills/` → embedded): agentskills.io standard. See `docs/skills/`.\\n\\n**Process Hardening** (`vtcode-process-hardening/`): Pre-main security (ptrace disable, core dump disable, env var removal). Exit codes: 5/6/7. See `docs/development/PROCESS_HARDENING.md`.\\n\\n## Code Style & Conventions\\n\\n### Code Style\\n\\n**Errors**: `anyhow::Result<T>` with `.with_context()`. Never `unwrap()`.\\n\\n**Unsafe Code**: Do not use `unsafe` blocks or `#[allow(unsafe_code)]` (including tests). Prefer safe std/crate APIs.\\n\\n**Config**: Never hardcode. Use `vtcode_core::config::constants`, `vtcode.toml`, or `docs/models.json`.\\n\\n**Naming**: `snake_case` functions/vars, `PascalCase` types. Descriptive names, early returns, 4-space indent.\\n\\n**Docs**: `.md` in `./docs/` only (`README.md` exception).\\n\\n### Rust Style (epage)\\n\\nBased on [epage's Rust Style Guide](https://epage.github.io/dev/rust-style/). Code is technical writing — lead with salient details (inverted pyramid), guide the reader through structure.\\n\\n#### Project Structure\\n\\n- **P-MOD**: Prefer `mod.rs` over `name.rs` when splitting into directories. Keeps modules atomic for browsing/renaming. Enforced via `clippy.self_named_module_files`.\\n- **P-DIR-MOD**: Directory root modules (`mod.rs`, `lib.rs`) only re-export. All definitions live in topically named files.\\n- **P-PRELUDE-MOD**: Prelude modules only re-export.\\n- **P-PATH-MOD**: Avoid `#[path]`; use standard module lookup.\\n- **P-API**: API should be a subset of file layout — a reader should navigate from API to source by path.\\n- **P-VISIBILITY**: Limit visibility to module-scope (no `pub(_)`), `pub(crate)`, or `pub`. If not fully abstracted within a module, use `pub(crate)`.\\n\\n#### File Structure\\n\\n- **M-PRIV-PUB-USE**: Private imports first, then public re-exports (group `pub use` with the public API).\\n- **M-PRIV-USE**: Limit private imports to heavily-used items where intent is clear from the name. Import traits anonymously (`use Trait as _`).\\n- **M-SINGLE-USE**: Import items individually, not compound (`use a::B; use a::C;` not `use a::{B, C};`).\\n- **M-ITEM-TOC**: Central/titular item first in a module — it provides context and a TOC for the rest.\\n- **M-TYPE-ASSOC**: Type definition immediately followed by its `impl` block, before the next type.\\n- **M-ASSOC-TRAIT**: Associated functions (`impl Foo {}`) before trait impls (`impl Display for Foo {}`).\\n- **M-CALLER-CALLEE**: Caller before callee. The weaker the callee's abstraction, the closer it should follow.\\n- **M-PUB-PRIV**: Public items before private items in modules, structs, and impl blocks.\\n\\n#### Function Structure\\n\\n- **F-GROUP**: Use blank lines to group related logic (\\\"paragraphs\\\" of a function).\\n- **F-OUT**: Open blocks with output variable declarations to announce intent.\\n- **F-VISUAL**: Blocks (`if`/`else`/`match`) should reflect business logic. Use early returns for bookkeeping, not business paths. Prefer combinators for non-business transformations.\\n- **F-PURE-MUT**: Pure expressions xor mutable statements — don't mix mutation with expression-based logic.\\n- **F-COMBINATOR**: Closures in combinators (`map`, `filter`, etc.) must be pure. Use `for` loops for side effects — `for_each`/`try_for_each` are disallowed via `clippy.toml`.\\n\\n### Logging & Tracing Guidelines\\n\\n**Architecture**: Shared buffered writer in `vtcode-core/src/utils/trace_writer.rs` (`FlushableWriter`) with flush hook in `vtcode-commons/src/trace_flush.rs`. Tracing setup is centralized in `src/main_helpers/tracing.rs` via `install_tracing_stack()`.\\n\\n**Level discipline** — choose the right level for each log:\\n- `error!` — Unrecoverable failures that need operator attention\\n- `warn!` — Degraded behavior (rate limits, circuit breakers, loop detection, config issues)\\n- `info!` — Key lifecycle milestones (session start/end, approval decisions, feature activation)\\n- `debug!` — Useful for troubleshooting (provider registration, cache hits, config loading)\\n- `trace!` — Per-invocation internals (tool routing, policy checks, safety gateway, CGP lifecycle, payload diagnostics)\\n\\n**Rules**:\\n- Never log at `info!` for per-tool-invocation events — use `trace!`\\n- Never log at `debug!` for routine happy-path checks (read-only classification, preapproval) — use `trace!`\\n- Keep `info!` for events that appear ≤ once per session phase (init, shutdown, mode change)\\n- Actionable logs only: if the reader can't act on it, downgrade or remove it\\n- No-op paths should be silent: early-return without logging when there's nothing to do (e.g., empty custom providers list)\\n\\n**Flush on exit**: Always call `vtcode_commons::trace_flush::flush_trace_log()` (or `vtcode_core::utils::trace_writer::flush_trace_log()`) before `process::exit()` or at shutdown. Signal handlers and TUI runner already do this.\\n\\n**Session file retention**: All session artifacts (`.json`, `.jsonl`, `.log`) are pruned by `session_archive` retention (default: 100 files, 14 days, 100 MB). Debug logs have separate rotation at 50 MB / 7 days.\\n\\n## Testing\\n\\n**Organization**: Unit tests inline (`#[cfg(test)]`), integration in `tests/`, benchmarks in `benches/`, snapshots via [`insta`](https://insta.rs).\\n\\n```bash\\n# Build & check\\ncargo build\\ncargo check\\n\\n# Test (nextest is 3-5x faster)\\ncargo nextest run              # All tests\\ncargo nextest run -p vtcode-core  # Single package\\ncargo nextest run --test integration_tests\\ncargo nextest run -- --nocapture\\n\\n# Quick profiles (aliases)\\ncargo t   # nextest run\\ncargo tq  # no retries\\ncargo ts  # fallback\\n\\n# Snapshots (insta)\\ncargo insta test    # Run + review\\ncargo insta review  # Interactive\\ncargo insta accept  # Accept all\\n\\n# Benchmarks\\ncargo bench\\n\\n# Quality gate\\ncargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check && cargo check && cargo nextest run\\n\\n# Memory Safety (Miri)\\n./scripts/check.sh miri  # Run local Miri check to detect Undefined Behavior\\n```\\n\\n## Development Notes\\n\\n**Security**: Validate paths (workspace boundaries), command allowlists, tool policies in `vtcode.toml`, human-in-the-loop approval.\\n\\n**Performance**: Single codegen unit, strict Clippy, no `expect_used`/`unwrap_used`.\\n\\n### Performance & Simplicity\\n\\n- Don't speculate about bottlenecks; measure with VT Code benchmarks, traces, or targeted timings before optimizing.\\n- Benchmark before tuning and keep before/after evidence for non-trivial performance claims.\\n- Prefer simple or brute-force approaches when scale is unproven or inputs are usually small.\\n- Avoid fancy algorithms unless measurements show they matter more than the simpler alternative.\\n- Choose data structures and layout first; once the data is right, the algorithm should usually become obvious.\\n\\n**Blocked Handoff Recovery**: When a run writes a blocked handoff, check `.vtcode/tasks/current_blocked.md` (and `.vtcode/tasks/blockers/`) for context, then resume with `vtcode --resume <session_id>`.\\n\\n**Hooks Noise Control**: Set `hooks.lifecycle.quiet_success_output = true` in `vtcode.toml` to suppress plain stdout for successful lifecycle hooks while retaining structured/failed output.\\n\\n**Interactive Session Commands**: See `docs/user-guide/interactive-mode.md` for full details.\\n\\n- `/terminal-setup` runs the guided terminal setup flow for multiline bindings (see `docs/guides/terminal-optimization.md`).\\n- `/vim`, `/vim on`, `/vim off` toggle Vim prompt editing; set `ui.vim_mode = true` in `vtcode.toml` to enable by default.\\n- `/suggest`, `/tasks`, `/jobs` open prompt suggestions, the TODO panel, and the jobs picker.\\n\\n**Update System**: See `docs/guides/UPDATE_SYSTEM.md` for the full workflow.\\n\\n- `vtcode update` installs updates; `vtcode update --check` checks only.\\n- `vtcode update --list` lists available versions; `vtcode update --list --limit <N>` lists more.\\n- `vtcode update --pin <version>` pins a release; `vtcode update --unpin` clears the pin.\\n- `vtcode update --channel <stable|beta|nightly>` switches release channels.\\n\\n**Exec Mode Automation**: See `docs/user-guide/exec-mode.md` for the full workflow.\\n\\n- `vtcode exec --json` streams JSONL events; `vtcode exec --events <path>` writes JSONL to a file/dir.\\n- `vtcode exec --last-message-file <path>` saves the final summary message.\\n- `vtcode exec resume <SESSION_ID> <PROMPT>` or `vtcode exec resume --last <PROMPT>` continues a run.\\n\\n**Exec Plans vs Plan Mode**: See `docs/harness/EXEC_PLANS.md` for the full workflow.\\n\\n- Exec plans live in `docs/harness/exec-plans/active/NNN-description.md` and move to `docs/harness/exec-plans/completed/` when done.\\n- Plan Mode session plans live in `.vtcode/plans/` and exit via the `exit_plan_mode` tool.\\n\\n## Agent-First CLI Invariants\\n\\n- Prefer machine-readable output for automation (`vtcode ask --output-format json`, `vtcode exec --json`).\\n- Introspect tool schemas at runtime before generating tool calls (`vtcode schema tools`).\\n- Use `vtcode exec --dry-run` before mutation-oriented autonomous tasks.\\n- Assume agent-generated input can be adversarial; keep input validation strict for CLI/MCP fields.\\n\\n**Pitfalls**:\\n\\n1. Don't assume paths — validate boundaries\\n2. Don't skip quality gate\\n3. Don't assume `RwLock` is faster — benchmark; `Mutex` often wins\\n\\n### Slash Commands & Inline List UI\\n\\n- Register new slash command metadata in `vtcode-core/src/ui/slash.rs` (`SLASH_COMMANDS`) so it appears in the `/` suggestion list.\\n- Wire command parsing and outcome routing end-to-end: `src/agent/runloop/slash_commands.rs` -> `src/agent/runloop/unified/turn/session/slash_commands/mod.rs` -> concrete handler in `.../slash_commands/ui.rs` (or relevant handler module).\\n- For picker/selection UX, use shared inline list flows (`ShowListModal` / `ShowWizardModal` / shared inline events) instead of introducing new popup/overlay widget implementations.\\n- Keep slash behavior consistent: if a command should execute immediately from slash selection, update the immediate-submit matcher in `vtcode-tui/src/core_tui/session/slash.rs`.\\n- Add focused tests when touching this path (at minimum: `vtcode-core` slash suggestion tests and `vtcode-tui` slash/session tests).\\n\\n## Agent Execution Guidelines\\n\\n## Summary Instructions\\n\\nWhen VT Code compacts or summarizes a conversation, preserve:\\n\\n- The current task objective and acceptance criteria\\n- File paths that were read or modified\\n- Test results and error messages\\n- Decisions made and the reasoning behind them\\n\\n### Task Execution\\n\\n- **Complete autonomously**: Resolve fully before yielding; no intermediate confirmations\\n- **Root cause fixes**: Fix at the source, not surface-level\\n- **Verify yourself**: Run `cargo check`, `cargo nextest`, `cargo clippy` after changes.\\n- **No scope creep**: Stick to the task; don't fix unrelated issues (mention in final message).\\n- **Testing**: Prefer cargo nextest for speed. Add tests if none exist, but don't add if it requires significant scope increase. Also run cargo insta snapshots if applicable for UI tests.\\n- **Safety gate**: No `unsafe` code (including tests) and enforce `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`\",\"content_kind\":\"text\",\"path\":\"AGENTS.md\",\"encoding\":\"utf8\"}",

      "role": "Tool",
      "content": "{\"content\":\"//! # VT Code - Terminal Coding Agent\\n//!\\n//! VT Code is a Rust-based terminal coding agent that pairs a streamlined\\n//! crossterm-powered interface with LLM-native code understanding and robust shell safety.\\n//! It is designed for developers who need precise context handling,\\n//! secure tool execution, and configurable multi-provider AI workflows.\\n//!\\n//! ## Highlights\\n//!\\n//! - **Multi-provider agent**: integrations for OpenAI, Anthropic,\\n//!   DeepSeek, Gemini, OpenRouter, and Ollama (local) with automatic failover and spend guards.\\n//! - **Semantic code understanding**: LLM-native code analysis and navigation (Rust, Python,\\n//!   JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, Java, and more) without the need for local grammar libraries.\\n//! - **Bash shell safety**: tree-sitter-bash integration for critical command validation\\n//!   and security enforcement.\\n//! - **Modern terminal experience**: inline renderer with streaming PTY output,\\n//!   slash commands, and customizable Ciapre-inspired theming.\\n//! - **Workspace-aware automation**: git-aware fuzzy navigation, workspace\\n//!   boundary enforcement, command allowlists, and human-in-the-loop\\n//!   confirmation.\\n//! - **Config-driven behavior**: every agent control lives in `vtcode.toml`,\\n//!   anchored by constants in `vtcode_core::config::constants` and curated model\\n//!   metadata in `docs/models.json`.\\n//!\\n//! ## Quickstart\\n//!\\n//! ```bash\\n//! # Install the CLI (cargo, npm, or Homebrew are also supported)\\n//! cargo install vtcode\\n//!\\n//! # Export the API key for your provider\\n//! export OPENAI_API_KEY=\\\"your-key\\\"\\n//!\\n//! # Launch the agent with explicit provider/model overrides\\n//! vtcode --provider openai --model gpt-5-codex\\n//!\\n//! # Run a one-off prompt with streaming output\\n//! vtcode ask \\\"Summarize diagnostics in src/lib.rs\\\"\\n//!\\n//! # Perform a dry run without tool execution\\n//! vtcode --no-tools ask \\\"Review recent changes in src/main.rs\\\"\\n//! ```\\n//!\\n//! Persist long-lived defaults in `vtcode.toml` instead of hardcoding them:\\n//!\\n//! ```toml\\n//! [agent]\\n//! provider = \\\"openai\\\"\\n//! default_model = \\\"gpt-5.4\\\"\\n//! ```\\n//!\\n//! The configuration loader resolves aliases through\\n//! `vtcode_core::config::constants`, while `docs/models.json` tracks the latest\\n//! vetted provider model identifiers.\\n//!\\n//! ## Architecture Overview\\n//!\\n//! VT Code separates reusable library components from the CLI entrypoint:\\n//!\\n//! - `vtcode-core/` exposes the agent runtime, provider abstractions (`llm/`),\\n//!   tool registry (`tools/`), configuration loaders, and shell safety\\n//!   integrations orchestrated with Tokio.\\n//! - `src/main.rs` embeds the inline UI, Clap-based CLI, and runtime wiring.\\n//! - MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools provide contextual resources (e.g.\\n//!   Serena MCP for journaling and memory), with policies expressed entirely in\\n//!   configuration.\\n//! - Safety features include workspace boundary enforcement, rate limiting,\\n//!   telemetry controls, and confirm-to-run guardrails.\\n//!\\n//! Additional implementation details live in `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and the\\n//! guides under `docs/project/`.\\n//!\\n//! ## Distribution Channels\\n//!\\n//! VT Code is distributed via multiple ecosystems:\\n//!\\n//! - **crates.io**: `cargo install vtcode`\\n//! - **npm**: `npm install -g vtcode`\\n//! - **Homebrew**: `brew install vtcode`\\n//! - **GitHub Releases**: pre-built binaries for macOS, Linux, and Windows\\n//!\\n//! ## crates.io Categories\\n//!\\n//! This crate is listed under the following crates.io categories to improve\\n//! discoverability for terminal-focused developer tooling:\\n//!\\n//! - [`#development-tools`](https://crates.io/categories/development-tools)\\n//! - [`#command-line-utilities`](https://crates.io/categories/command-line-utilities)\\n//!\\n//! ## Library Usage Examples\\n//!\\n//! ### Starting an Agent Programmatically\\n//!\\n//! ```rust,ignore\\n//! use vtcode_core::{Agent, VTCodeConfig};\\n//!\\n//! #[tokio::main]\\n//! async fn main() -> Result<(), anyhow::Error> {\\n//!     let config = VTCodeConfig::load()?;\\n//!     let agent = Agent::new(config).await?;\\n//!     agent.run().await?;\\n//!     Ok(())\\n//! }\\n//! ```\\n//!\\n//! ### Registering a Custom Tool\\n//!\\n//! ```rust,ignore\\n//! use vtcode_core::tools::{ToolRegistry, ToolRegistration};\\n//!\\n//! #[tokio::main]\\n//! async fn main() -> Result<(), anyhow::Error> {\\n//!     let workspace = std::env::current_dir()?;\\n//!     let mut registry = ToolRegistry::new(workspace);\\n//!\\n//!     let custom_tool = ToolRegistration {\\n//!         name: \\\"my_custom_tool\\\".into(),\\n//!         description: \\\"A custom tool for specific tasks\\\".into(),\\n//!         parameters: serde_json::json!({\\n//!             \\\"type\\\": \\\"object\\\",\\n//!             \\\"properties\\\": {\\n//!                 \\\"input\\\": {\\\"type\\\": \\\"string\\\"}\\n//!             }\\n//!         }),\\n//!         handler: |args| async move {\\n//!             // Tool implementation goes here\\n//!             Ok(serde_json::json!({\\\"result\\\": \\\"success\\\"}))\\n//!         },\\n//!     };\\n//!\\n//!     registry.register_tool(custom_tool).await?;\\n//!     Ok(())\\n//! }\\n//! ```\\n//!\\n//! ## Agent Client Protocol (ACP)\\n//!\\n//! VT Code ships an ACP bridge tailored for Zed. Enable it via the `[acp]` section in\\n//! `vtcode.toml`, launch `vtcode acp`, and register the binary inside Zed's\\n//! `agent_servers` list. The full walkthrough lives in the\\n//! [Zed ACP integration guide](https://github.com/vinhnx/vtcode/blob/main/docs/guides/zed-acp.md),\\n//! and the rendered API guarantees appear on\\n//! [docs.rs](https://docs.rs/vtcode/latest/vtcode/#agent-client-protocol-acp).\\n//!\\n//! ### Bridge guarantees\\n//!\\n//! - Filesystem tooling stays disabled unless Zed advertises `fs.read_text_file`, so the agent\\n//!   never emits unsupported requests.\\n//! - Each `read_file` call is wrapped in `session/request_permission`, letting you approve or\\n//!   deny access to sensitive paths before any data leaves the editor.\\n//! - Cancellation signals from Zed halt streaming, mark pending tools as cancelled, and end the\\n//!   turn with `StopReason::Cancelled` for clean transcripts.\\n//! - ACP `plan` updates broadcast analysis, optional context gathering, and response drafting\\n//!   progress so Zed mirrors the agent's workflow.\\n//! - Strict absolute-path validation blocks relative or out-of-workspace arguments before they\\n//!   reach the client.\\n//! - When the model lacks tool calling, VT Code emits reasoning notices and downgrades to plain\\n//!   completions while keeping the plan timeline consistent.\\n\\n//!\\n//! VT Code binary package\\n//!\\n//! This package contains the binary executable for VT Code.\\n//! For the core library functionality, see [`vtcode-core`](https://docs.rs/vtcode-core).\\n\\npub use vtcode_acp as acp;\\n\\npub mod config_watcher {\\n    pub use vtcode_config::loader::watch::*;\\n}\\npub use vtcode_tui::ui::interactive_list;\",\"content_kind\":\"text\",\"path\":\"src/lib.rs\",\"encoding\":\"utf8\"}",
      "tool_call_id": "call_Ig0Fzgx8lZ26HZUASEcfKHnq"
    },
    {
      "role": "Tool",
      "content": "{\"content\":\"//! # vtcode-core - Runtime for VT Code\\n//!\\n//! `vtcode-core` powers the VT Code terminal coding agent. It provides the\\n//! reusable building blocks for multi-provider LLM orchestration, tool\\n//! execution, semantic code analysis, and configurable safety policies.\\n//!\\n//! ## Highlights\\n//!\\n//! - **Provider Abstraction**: unified LLM interface with adapters for OpenAI,\\n//!   Anthropic, xAI, DeepSeek, Gemini, OpenRouter, and Ollama (local), including automatic\\n//!   failover and spend controls.\\n//! - **Prompt Caching**: cross-provider prompt caching system that leverages\\n//!   provider-specific caching capabilities (OpenAI's automatic caching, Anthropic's\\n//!   cache_control blocks, Gemini's implicit/explicit caching) to reduce costs and\\n//!   latency, with configurable settings per provider.\\n//! - **Semantic Workspace Model**: LLM-native code analysis and navigation\\n//!   across all modern programming languages.\\n//! - **Bash Shell Safety**: tree-sitter-bash integration for critical command validation\\n//!   and security enforcement.\\n//! - **Tool System**: trait-driven registry for shell execution, file IO,\\n//!   search, and custom commands, with Tokio-powered concurrency and PTY\\n//!   streaming.\\n//! - **Configuration-First**: everything is driven by `vtcode.toml`, with\\n//!   model, safety, and automation constants centralized in\\n//!   `config::constants` and curated metadata in `docs/models.json`.\\n//! - **Safety & Observability**: workspace boundary enforcement, command\\n//!   allow/deny lists, human-in-the-loop confirmation, and structured event\\n//!   logging for comprehensive audit trails.\\n//!\\n//! ## Architecture Overview\\n//!\\n//! The crate is organized into several key modules:\\n//!\\n//! - `config/`: configuration loader, defaults, and schema validation.\\n//! - `llm/`: provider clients, request shaping, and response handling.\\n//! - `tools/`: built-in tool implementations plus registration utilities.\\n//! - `context/`: conversation management and memory.\\n//! - `executor/`: async orchestration for tool invocations and streaming output.\\n//! - `core/prompt_caching`: cross-provider prompt caching system that leverages\\n//!   provider-specific caching mechanisms for cost optimization and reduced latency.\\n//!\\n//! ## Quickstart\\n//!\\n//! ```rust,ignore\\n//! use vtcode_core::{Agent, VTCodeConfig};\\n//!\\n//! #[tokio::main]\\n//! async fn main() -> Result<(), anyhow::Error> {\\n//!     // Load configuration from vtcode.toml or environment overrides\\n//!     let config = VTCodeConfig::load()?;\\n//!\\n//!     // Construct the agent runtime\\n//!     let agent = Agent::new(config).await?;\\n//!\\n//!     // Execute an interactive session\\n//!     agent.run().await?;\\n//!\\n//!     Ok(())\\n//! }\\n//! ```\\n//!\\n//! ## Extending VT Code\\n//!\\n//! Register custom tools or providers by composing the existing traits:\\n//!\\n//! ```rust,ignore\\n//! use vtcode_core::tools::{ToolRegistry, ToolRegistration};\\n//!\\n//! #[tokio::main]\\n//! async fn main() -> Result<(), anyhow::Error> {\\n//!     let workspace = std::env::current_dir()?;\\n//!     let mut registry = ToolRegistry::new(workspace);\\n//!\\n//!     let custom_tool = ToolRegistration {\\n//!         name: \\\"my_custom_tool\\\".into(),\\n//!         description: \\\"A custom tool for specific tasks\\\".into(),\\n//!         parameters: serde_json::json!({\\n//!             \\\"type\\\": \\\"object\\\",\\n//!             \\\"properties\\\": { \\\"input\\\": { \\\"type\\\": \\\"string\\\" } }\\n//!         }),\\n//!         handler: |_args| async move {\\n//!             // Implement your tool behavior here\\n//!             Ok(serde_json::json!({ \\\"result\\\": \\\"success\\\" }))\\n//!         },\\n//!     };\\n//!\\n//!     registry.register_tool(custom_tool).await?;\\n//!     Ok(())\\n//! }\\n//! ```\\n//!\\n//! For a complete tour of modules and extension points, read\\n//! `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and the guides in `docs/project/`.\\n//!\\n//! ## Agent Client Protocol (ACP)\\n//!\\n//! VT Code's binary exposes an ACP bridge for Zed. Enable it via the `[acp]` section in\\n//! `vtcode.toml`, launch the `vtcode acp` subcommand, and register the binary under\\n//! `agent_servers` in Zed's `settings.json`. Detailed instructions and troubleshooting live in the\\n//! [Zed ACP integration guide](https://github.com/vinhnx/vtcode/blob/main/docs/guides/zed-acp.md),\\n//! with a rendered summary on\\n//! [docs.rs](https://docs.rs/vtcode/latest/vtcode/#agent-client-protocol-acp).\\n\\n//! ### Bridge guarantees\\n//!\\n//! - Tool exposure follows capability negotiation: `read_file` stays disabled unless Zed\\n//!   advertises `fs.read_text_file`.\\n//! - Each filesystem request invokes `session/request_permission`, ensuring explicit approval\\n//!   within the editor before data flows.\\n//! - Cancellation signals propagate into VT Code, cancelling active tool calls and ending the\\n//!   turn with `StopReason::Cancelled`.\\n//! - ACP `plan` entries track analysis, context gathering, and response drafting for timeline\\n//!   parity with Zed.\\n//! - Absolute-path checks guard every `read_file` argument before forwarding it to the client.\\n//! - Non-tool-capable models trigger reasoning notices and an automatic downgrade to plain\\n//!   completions without losing plan consistency.\\n\\n//!\\n//! VT Code Core Library\\n//!\\n//! This crate provides the core functionality for the VT Code agent,\\n//! including tool implementations, LLM integration, and utility functions.\\n\\n// Public modules\\npub mod a2a; // Agent2Agent Protocol support\\npub mod acp;\\n#[cfg(feature = \\\"anthropic-api\\\")]\\npub mod anthropic_api; // Compatibility facade; canonical implementation lives under llm/providers/anthropic\\npub mod audit;\\npub mod auth; // OAuth PKCE authentication for providers\\npub mod cache; // Unified caching system\\npub mod cli;\\npub mod code;\\npub mod command_safety; // Command safety detection (Codex patterns)\\npub mod commands;\\npub mod compaction;\\npub mod components; // Context-Generic Programming (CGP) substrate for composable tool runtimes\\npub mod config;\\npub mod constants;\\npub mod context; // Vibe coding support: entity resolution, workspace state, conversation memory\\npub mod copilot;\\npub mod core;\\npub mod diagnostics;\\npub mod dotfile_protection; // Comprehensive dotfile protection system\\npub mod error; // Structured error handling\\npub mod exec;\\npub mod exec_policy; // Codex-style execution policy management\\n/// Backward-compatible alias: command-level validation now lives in `exec_policy::command_validation`.\\npub use exec_policy::command_validation as execpolicy;\\npub mod gemini; // Compatibility facade; canonical internal import path is llm/providers/gemini::wire\\npub mod git_info; // Git repository information collection\\npub mod hooks;\\npub mod http_client;\\npub mod ide_context;\\npub mod instructions;\\npub mod llm;\\npub mod marketplace;\\npub mod mcp;\\npub mod memory; // Memory monitoring and pressure detection\\npub mod metrics;\\npub mod models;\\npub mod models_manager; // Models discovery, caching, and selection (Codex patterns)\\npub mod notifications;\\npub mod open_responses; // Open Responses specification conformance layer\\npub mod orchestrator;\\npub mod permissions;\\npub mod plugins;\\npub mod pods;\\npub mod project_doc;\\npub mod prompts;\\npub mod retry;\\npub mod review;\\npub mod safety;\\npub mod sandboxing; // Codex-style sandbox policy and execution environment\\npub mod security;\\npub mod session;\\npub mod shutdown;\\npub mod skills;\\npub mod subagents;\\npub mod telemetry;\\npub mod terminal_setup;\\npub mod tool_policy;\\npub mod tools;\\npub mod trace; // Agent Trace specification for AI code attribution\\npub mod turn_metadata; // Turn metadata for LLM requests (git context)\\npub mod types;\\npub mod ui;\\npub mod utils;\\nmod zsh_exec_bridge;\\n\\n//       "content": "{\"content\":\"use anyhow::{Context, Result};\\nuse std::path::Path;\\nuse vtcode_core::utils::error_log_collector::ErrorLogCollectorLayer;\\nuse vtcode_core::utils::session_debug::{\\n    DEFAULT_MAX_DEBUG_LOG_AGE_DAYS, DEFAULT_MAX_DEBUG_LOG_SIZE_MB, current_debug_session_id,\\n    prepare_debug_log_file, set_runtime_debug_log_path,\\n};\\nuse vtcode_core::utils::trace_writer::FlushableWriter;\\nuse vtcode_tui::log::{is_tui_log_capture_enabled, make_tui_log_layer};\\n\\nfn maybe_tui_log_layer() -> Option<vtcode_tui::log::TuiLogLayer> {\\n    if is_tui_log_capture_enabled() {\\n        Some(make_tui_log_layer())\\n    } else {\\n        None\\n    }\\n}\\n\\n/// Build the common tracing stack: buffered file writer + TUI layer + error collector.\\n///\\n/// Returns `Ok(())` on success, or an error if subscriber init fails.\\nfn install_tracing_stack(log_file: &Path, env_filter: tracing_subscriber::EnvFilter) -> Result<()> {\\n    use tracing_subscriber::{fmt::format::FmtSpan, prelude::*};\\n\\n    set_runtime_debug_log_path(log_file);\\n    let writer = FlushableWriter::open(log_file)\\n        .with_context(|| format!(\\\"Failed to open trace log: {}\\\", log_file.display()))?;\\n\\n    let fmt_layer = tracing_subscriber::fmt::layer()\\n        .with_writer(move || writer.clone())\\n        .with_span_events(FmtSpan::FULL)\\n        .with_ansi(false);\\n\\n    tracing_subscriber::registry()\\n        .with(env_filter)\\n        .with(fmt_layer)\\n        .with(maybe_tui_log_layer())\\n        .with(ErrorLogCollectorLayer)\\n        .try_init()\\n        .map_err(|err| anyhow::anyhow!(\\\"{err}\\\"))\\n}\\n\\npub(crate) async fn initialize_tracing() -> Result<bool> {\\n    if std::env::var(\\\"RUST_LOG\\\").is_ok() {\\n        let env_filter = tracing_subscriber::EnvFilter::from_default_env();\\n        let session_id = current_debug_session_id();\\n        let log_file = prepare_debug_log_file(\\n            None,\\n            &session_id,\\n            DEFAULT_MAX_DEBUG_LOG_SIZE_MB,\\n            DEFAULT_MAX_DEBUG_LOG_AGE_DAYS,\\n        )?;\\n\\n        if let Err(err) = install_tracing_stack(&log_file, env_filter) {\\n            tracing::warn!(error = %err, \\\"tracing already initialized; skipping env tracing setup\\\");\\n        }\\n\\n        return Ok(true);\\n    }\\n\\n    Ok(false)\\n}\\n\\npub(crate) fn initialize_default_error_tracing() -> Result<()> {\\n    use tracing_subscriber::prelude::*;\\n\\n    let env_filter = tracing_subscriber::EnvFilter::new(\\\"error\\\");\\n\\n    let init_result = tracing_subscriber::registry()\\n        .with(env_filter)\\n        .with(ErrorLogCollectorLayer)\\n        .try_init();\\n\\n    if let Err(err) = init_result {\\n        tracing::warn!(error = %err, \\\"tracing already initialized; skipping default error tracing setup\\\");\\n    }\\n\\n    Ok(())\\n}\\n\\npub(crate) fn initialize_tracing_from_config(\\n    config: &vtcode_core::config::loader::VTCodeConfig,\\n) -> Result<()> {\\n    let debug_cfg = &config.debug;\\n    let targets = if debug_cfg.trace_targets.is_empty() {\\n        \\\"vtcode_core,vtcode\\\".to_string()\\n    } else {\\n        debug_cfg.trace_targets.join(\\\",\\\")\\n    };\\n\\n    let filter_str = format!(\\\"{}={}\\\", targets, debug_cfg.trace_level.as_str());\\n\\n    let env_filter = tracing_subscriber::EnvFilter::try_from_default_env()\\n        .unwrap_or_else(|_| tracing_subscriber::EnvFilter::new(&filter_str));\\n\\n    let configured_dir = debug_cfg\\n        .debug_log_dir\\n        .as_ref()\\n        .map(|_| debug_cfg.debug_log_path());\\n    let session_id = current_debug_session_id();\\n    let log_file = prepare_debug_log_file(\\n        configured_dir,\\n        &session_id,\\n        debug_cfg.max_debug_log_size_mb,\\n        debug_cfg.max_debug_log_age_days,\\n    )?;\\n\\n    match install_tracing_stack(&log_file, env_filter) {\\n        Ok(()) => {\\n            tracing::info!(\\n                \\\"Debug tracing enabled: targets={}, level={}, log_file={}\\\",\\n                targets,\\n                debug_cfg.trace_level,\\n                log_file.display()\\n            );\\n        }\\n        Err(err) => {\\n            tracing::warn!(\\n                error = %err,\\n                \\\"tracing already initialized; skipping config tracing setup\\\"\\n            );\\n        }\\n    }\\n\\n    Ok(())\\n}\",\"path\":\"src/main_helpers/tracing.rs\",\"content_kind\":\"text\",\"encoding\":\"utf8\"}",
````
